source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Identify and compare the suitability of a variety of everyday materials for particular uses.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack teaches material choice by testing properties in playful design challenges.

objective.child_goal: I can choose a material because of its properties.
objective.statement: Compare everyday materials and explain why a material is suitable or unsuitable for a use.
misconceptions: Chooses a material because it looks nice rather than because it has useful properties.
Thinks one material is always best for every job.
Confuses the object with the material it is made from.

vocabulary.child_explanation: What something is made from, such as wood, metal, plastic or fabric.
vocabulary.audio_script: A material is what an object is made from.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Object cards flipping to material labels.

vocabulary.audio_script: Suitable means it works well for the job.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A material card locking into a design challenge.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Is the spoon the object or the material?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The spoon is the object. Metal is the material.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Object cards flip to show material labels.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose a material for a rainy-day roof.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce suitability as matching properties to a use.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A good roof material should keep water out.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A small shelter waits for a roof material.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Plastic is suitable for the roof because it is waterproof.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model because explanation using a property.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Plastic works because water does not soak through it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Raindrops bounce off plastic and soak into paper.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort the materials by flexible, strong or waterproof.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise matching properties to material evidence.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Look at what the material can do.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Material cards move into property test stations.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which material is best for a window?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A window needs a material you can see through.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Glass, fabric and wood cards are tested for transparency.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Pip why paper is not the best roof in the rain.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair appearance-based choice with property reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I like the paper colour. Why should I test it?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion watches paper sag under raindrops.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners test properties and choose materials for a purpose.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners run simple water, bend, strength and transparency tests before making a choice.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Audio descriptions, clear icon labels, reduced-motion before/after panels and no failure shaming.

manipulatives.purpose: Help learners group materials by useful properties before matching more than one possible material to a design job.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners tap material cards into labelled crates such as waterproof, flexible, transparent or strong, then open a mystery job card and choose using evidence from the crates.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Supports keyboard and single-switch card selection, tap-to-place and audio labels; static mode uses a clear card-and-table layout, while reduced-motion mode replaces crate movement with instant placement and text confirmation.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave property sorting and design choices after object/material vocabulary is secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Separate object names from material names.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: object image + material labels + flip reveal

variant_blueprints.purpose: Observe waterproof, strong, flexible and transparent properties.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: material card + test station + observation result

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose material for a use and explain with a property.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: design challenge + three materials + because choices
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Distractors should be attractive but unsuitable where useful.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Show that suitability depends on purpose.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same material + two different uses + compare suitability
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid suggesting any material is globally good or bad.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain material-property-use links through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: quick use/property prompt from review queue

body.prompt: Which material is suitable for a rainy-day roof?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: A roof should keep water out.
Which material is waterproof?
question_variants.explanation: Plastic is suitable because it is waterproof.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "plastic sheet". Plastic is suitable because it is waterproof.
feedback.repair: A roof should keep water out. Which material is waterproof? Use that clue to repair appearance choice, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this material test, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "plastic sheet": Plastic is suitable because it is waterproof.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: Why is glass suitable for a window?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: A window should let us see through it.
Transparent means see-through.
question_variants.explanation: Glass is suitable for many windows because it is transparent.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "It is transparent". Glass is suitable for many windows because it is transparent.
feedback.repair: A window should let us see through it. Transparent means see-through. Use that clue to repair property use mismatch, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this explain choice, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "It is transparent": Glass is suitable for many windows because it is transparent.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

feedback.correct: Evidence matched: the property supports the job or conclusion.
feedback.repair: The useful evidence is the property: soft and squashy.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Chooses a material because it looks nice rather than because it has useful properties.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects attractive but unsuitable material.
repair_sequence: Compare test result with the job.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner chooses a suitable material and cites a property.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses the object with the material it is made from.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says the material is 'spoon' or 'chair'.
repair_sequence: Flip object card to material layer.
Match a new object to material.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner identifies material for a new object.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm lab animations and no dramatic break effects.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate object, material and property labels with replay.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use icons for waterproof, strong, flexible and transparent.
adaptive_support.attention_support: One property test at a time.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Frame choices as testing designs, not wrong answers.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach material, object, waterproof, strong, flexible, transparent and suitable.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires matching materials to uses with simple property reasons; secure requires explaining why suitability changes with purpose.
evidence.parent_summary: Can choose a material for a job and explain the choice using properties.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Identifies materials, observes properties and gives because explanations for suitability.
next_actions: Interleave material names with property tests.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 2 materials pack with a deterministic 200-item pilot bank spanning object/material vocabulary, fair property-test evidence, property-based design choices, purpose-dependent suitability and spaced retrieval. Generated candidates require independent science, SEND, safeguarding, renderer and classroom review before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
